pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of sorting algorithms, specifically dealing with a limited range of values (0, 1, 2). The signals for using a specialized sorting technique arise from the fact that we only have three distinct values to sort, allowing for more efficient algorithms than general-purpose sorting methods.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Use a general-purpose sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort to sort the array.
approaches.algorithm: Sort the array using a built-in sorting function or a standard sorting algorithm.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize the Dutch National Flag algorithm to sort the colors in a single pass with constant space.
approaches.algorithm: Maintain three pointers to partition the array into three sections for 0s, 1s, and 2s.

dry_run: Given the input array [2, 0, 2, 1, 1, 0]: Start with three pointers: low = 0, mid = 0, high = 5. Iterate through the array: If nums[mid] is 0, swap with nums[low] and increment both low and mid. If nums[mid] is 1, just increment mid. If nums[mid] is 2, swap with nums[high] and decrement high. Continue until mid exceeds high. The sorted array will be [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2].
edge_cases: An empty input array should return an empty array. A single element array (e.g., [1]) should return the same array. Arrays with duplicates (e.g., [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2]) should be handled correctly. Large input arrays should be sorted efficiently without performance degradation.
common_pitfalls: Common mistakes include: 1) Not maintaining the correct pointers leading to incorrect swaps. 2) Failing to handle the case where mid pointer exceeds high, causing an infinite loop. 3) Using a sorting algorithm that does not operate in-place, resulting in additional space usage.
